Name: rate-distortion-guided_quantization
Title: Rate-Distortion-Guided Quantization for Neural Networks: Bridging Theory and Practice
Short Hypothesis: By directly optimizing quantization levels to minimize the rate-distortion function, we can achieve more efficient compression while maintaining task performance, compared to heuristic-based quantization methods.
Related Work: Recent works like 'Neural Estimation of the Rate-Distortion Function' and 'Estimating the Rate-Distortion Function by Wasserstein Gradient Descent' focus on estimating the rate-distortion function but do not use it to guide quantization. Existing quantization methods (e.g., AMP-ViT, EMQ) rely on heuristics or proxies rather than a principled information-theoretic approach. This proposal distinguishes itself by directly optimizing quantization levels using the rate-distortion function, providing a theoretical foundation for practical compression.
Abstract: Quantization is a key technique for compressing neural networks, but existing methods often rely on heuristics or proxies that lack theoretical guarantees. This paper proposes a principled approach to quantization by optimizing quantization levels to minimize the rate-distortion function, which characterizes the fundamental trade-off between compression rate and task performance. We introduce a framework that estimates the rate-distortion function for a given task and uses it to guide quantization decisions. Experiments on image classification and language models demonstrate that our method achieves better compression-performance trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art quantization methods. By bridging information theory and practical compression, this work provides a new direction for efficient neural network deployment.
Experiments: Estimate the rate-distortion function for image classification (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and language models (BERT, GPT-2) using neural estimators.
Optimize quantization levels using gradient-based methods to minimize the rate-distortion function, ensuring alignment with task-specific objectives.
Compare the proposed method with state-of-the-art quantization techniques (e.g., AMP-ViT, EMQ) in terms of compression ratio, accuracy, and inference speed.
Analyze the gap between rate-distortion-optimal quantization and heuristic-based methods, quantifying the redundancy in existing quantization schemes.
Evaluate the robustness of the proposed method to different architectures (CNNs, Transformers) and tasks (classification, generation).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating the rate-distortion function for high-dimensional data may be computationally expensive.
The proposed method assumes a known distortion measure aligned with the task, which may not always be straightforward to define.
The benefits of rate-distortion-guided quantization may diminish for very low-bit quantization (e.g., 1-bit), where heuristic methods are already highly optimized.
The method may require task-specific tuning, limiting its generalizability across diverse applications.
